title: Evidence chains are only useful if they stay readable
summary: An essay on evidence chains, readability, and why proof loses value when nobody can follow it.
topic: evidence chains when reliability turned out to be the whole game
body: I wrote this down in June 2025 after thinking about evidence chains from the vantage point of an office where the exciting part was no longer the model but the glue. I was less interested in the headline claim than in the older question beneath it, what kind of world does this thing presume is already waiting? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. Most discussions rush from capability to performance to scale and leave the question of livability trailing behind. Evidence chains seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The public version kept pointing at one mechanism, but it was another one that felt socially decisive to me. Public language tends to grab the most visible element first: the model, the dashboard, the robot, the bit that photographs well. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They quietly decide who may stay general and who must become machine-readable. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, evidence chains stop looking like a gadget and start looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. Because it resists tidy measurement, that difference tends to vanish from the official accounting. But mood is one of the outputs. A system that steadily constricts attention or multiplies little permissions is telling you something honest about itself. It is part of what the product is. I do not buy the move where human texture gets treated as a decorative concern beside performance.

Looking back from June 2025, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “The problem with evaluation is usually what happens when a product grows more competent than legible,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. What held me here was the post-demo layer: upkeep, political assumptions, quiet social sorting, and the emotional terms on which people were being asked to cooperate with the system. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was incomplete in the familiar way optimism becomes incomplete before the machinery has touched enough real lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. Good tools deserve scrutiny too, especially about the kind of world they make easier to inhabit. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. The healthy move is to keep the claim narrow until something real forces it to widen. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “What research theater taught me about building futures.”

A final note to myself from June 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. These days I care less about winning the prediction game than about noticing what a tool asks of the people standing closest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

references.title: Most evaluation problems are really trust problems
What research theater taught me about building futures
